items.title: The Ultimate 2025 Linux Troubleshooting & Security Forensics Guide (High-level Commands for SRE…
items.author: Harikrishnan M
items.content:
The Ultimate 2025 Linux Troubleshooting & Security Forensics Guide (High-level Commands for SRE, DevOps & SecOps)

🚀 Performance & Process Analysis

Process Monitoring Deep Dive

# Real-time process analysistop -p PID1,PID2,PID3              # Monitor specific processes by PID
htop --tree                         # Visual process tree representation
pidstat 2 5                        # Detailed process stats every 2s for 5 iterations
ps aux --sort=-%mem | head -10     # Top 10 memory hogs
ps -eo pid,ppid,cmd,%mem,%cpu --sort=-%cpu | head -20  # Custom process output
pstree -p -a -A                    # ASCII process tree with PIDs and arguments
# What these tell you:# - Resource bottlenecks (CPU, memory, I/O)
# - Process hierarchies and parent-child relationships
# - Zombie processes and orphaned tasks

Advanced Performance Diagnostics

# CPU and memory analysismpstat -P ALL 1                    # Per-core CPU utilization breakdown
sar -u 1 5                         # System Activity Reporter - CPU history
vmstat 1 10                        # Virtual memory, blocks, and CPU activity
free -h -s 5                       # Human-readable memory usage every 5s
slabtop -o                         # Kernel slab cache memory usage
numastat -m                        # NUMA memory allocation statistics
# I/O Subsystemiostat -x 1 10                     # Extended I/O statistics with utilization
iotop -o                           # Real-time I/O usage by process
lsof +D /var/log                   # All open files in directory
fuser -v /mnt/database             # Processes using specific filesystem
# Key insights:# - CPU steal time (virtualization overhead)
# - Memory pressure and swap activity
# - I/O wait times and disk saturation
# - Kernel memory allocation patterns

🌐 Network Troubleshooting Mastery

Connection State Analysis

# Comprehensive socket diagnosticsss -tulnpe                         # All listening sockets with processes and extended info
ss -s                              # Socket statistics summary
netstat -tunlp                     # Traditional socket listing with PID/program
netstat -s                         # Network stack statistics (packets, errors, drops)
lsof -i :80                        # Processes using port 80
lsof -i tcp                        # All TCP connections
# Advanced socket filteringss -o state established '( dport = :443 or sport = :443 )'  # Active HTTPS connections
ss -ltp                            # Listening TCP sockets with processes
# Critical for:# - Port conflicts and service binding issues
# - Connection state problems (TIME_WAIT accumulation)
# - Identifying processes behind network activity

Packet Analysis & Routing

# Traffic capture and analysistcpdump -i any -n port 53          # Capture DNS traffic on all interfaces
tcpdump -i eth0 -w capture.pcap    # Save packet capture to file
tshark -i eth0 -f "port 80"        # Wireshark CLI for HTTP analysis
tcpflow -c port 8080               # Reconstruct TCP streams for debugging
# Network path analysismtr -w 8.8.8.8                     # Continuous traceroute with statistics
tracepath google.com               # Path MTU discovery
ip route show table all            # Display all routing tables
ip rule show                       # Routing policy database
# Essential for:# - Network latency and packet loss diagnosis
# - Routing issues and asymmetric paths
# - Application protocol debugging

🔒 Security & Audit Framework

# Linux Audit Framework (auditd)ausearch -k failed-login           # Search audit logs for specific key
aureport -l -i                     # Comprehensive login report
auditctl -l                        # List active audit rules
ausearch -ts today -m USER_LOGIN   # Today's login events
# Security scanning and verificationrpm -Va                            # Verify integrity of all RPM packages
dpkg --verify apache2              # Debian package verification
chkrootkit                         # Rootkit detection scan
rkhunter --check                   # Comprehensive rootkit and backdoor scan
# These commands:# - Track security policy violations
# - Detect package tampering
# - Identify rootkits and compromises

File Integrity & Change Monitoring

# File system change detectionfind /etc -type f -mtime -1        # Files modified in last 24 hours
find /var/log -name "*.log" -size +100M  # Large log files
ls -laZ /etc/passwd                # View SELinux security context
stat /etc/shadow                   # Detailed file metadata and timestamps
# Integrity verificationmd5sum /sbin/init                  # System binary integrity check
sha256sum /bin/bash                # Cryptographic hash verification
cmp /bin/ls /backup/ls             # Byte-by-byte file comparison
# Critical for:# - Detecting unauthorized file changes
# - Security incident investigation
# - Compliance and audit requirements

🛡️ Iptables & Firewall Expertise

# Comprehensive iptables inspectioniptables -L -n -v --line-numbers   # List all rules with counters and line numbers
iptables -t nat -L -v              # NAT table rules with verbosity
iptables-save > firewall.rules     # Backup current firewall state
iptables-restore < firewall.rules  # Restore firewall rules
# Advanced rule examplesiptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -m state --state NEW -m recent --set --name SSH
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -m state --state NEW -m recent --update --seconds 60 --hitcount 4 --name SSH -j DROP
# These techniques:# - Implement SSH brute force protection
# - Provide connection rate limiting
# - Enable stateful firewall tracking

Advanced Firewall Techniques

# Rate limiting and DDoS protectioniptables -I INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -i eth0 -m state --state NEW -m limit --limit 10/minute --limit-burst 20 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j DROP
# Connection tracking and analysisconntrack -L                       # View connection tracking table
conntrack -E                       # Real-time connection events
conntrack -D -s 192.168.1.100      # Delete connections from specific IP
# Essential for:# - DDoS mitigation and rate limiting
# - Network forensics and connection analysis
# - Stateful firewall troubleshooting

⚙️ System & Kernel Diagnostics

Kernel and Hardware Analysis

# Kernel parameters and modulessysctl -a | grep tcp               # TCP/IP kernel parameters
lsmod | grep bridge                # Check loaded kernel modules
modinfo e1000                      # Network driver information
dmesg -T | tail -50                # Recent kernel messages with timestamps
journalctl -k -f                   # Follow kernel messages in real-time
# Hardware informationlscpu                              # Detailed CPU architecture info
lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,TYPE,MOUNTPOINT # Block devices with mount points
lspci -v | grep -i network         # Network hardware details
dmidecode -t memory                # RAM configuration and information
# Critical for:# - Kernel parameter optimization
# - Hardware compatibility issues
# - Driver and module problems

Storage and Filesystem Analysis

# Disk and filesystem diagnosticsdf -hT                             # Filesystem space, type, and usage
du -sh /var/* | sort -hr           # Directory sizes sorted by usage
lsblk -f                           # Block devices with filesystem types
mount | grep ^/dev                 # Currently mounted filesystems
smartctl -a /dev/sda               # Disk health and SMART data
# Inode and file handlingfind / -xdev -printf '%h\n' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10  # Directories with most files
df -i                              # Inode usage per filesystem
# Essential for:# - Storage capacity planning
# - Filesystem corruption detection
- Disk failure prediction

📊 Log Analysis & Anomaly Detection

Comprehensive Log Monitoring

# Real-time log analysistail -f /var/log/syslog | grep -i error
multitail /var/log/syslog /var/log/auth.log  # Monitor multiple logs simultaneously
journalctl -f -p err              # Follow systemd error messages
# Pattern detection and analysisgrep -c "Failed password" /var/log/auth.log  # Count failed login attempts
awk '/Invalid user/ {print $8}' /var/log/auth.log | sort | uniq -c  # Invalid usernames count
zgrep "session opened" /var/log/auth.log*    # Search compressed log files
# These commands help:# - Identify security breaches
# - Detect system errors and failures
# - Track user activity and access patterns

Security Anomaly Detection

# User and session analysislast -i                           # Login history with IP addresses
lastb -a                          # Failed login attempts with hostnames
who /var/run/utmp                 # Currently logged in users
w                                 # Who is logged in and what they're doing
# Account and privilege auditinggetent passwd | wc -l             # Count total user accounts
getent group                      # List all groups
awk -F: '($3 == 0) {print $1}' /etc/passwd  # Users with UID 0 (root)
# Critical for:# - Unauthorized access detection
# - Privilege escalation attempts
# - Account management issues

🐳 Container & Application Troubleshooting

# Apache/Nginx troubleshootingapachectl -t                      # Test Apache configuration syntax
nginx -t                          # Test Nginx configuration
apachectl -S                      # Show virtual host configuration
netstat -nap | grep :80           # Verify what's listening on port 80
# Database performancemysqladmin processlist            # MySQL active queries and connections
pg_stat_activity                  # PostgreSQL active queries
mongostat                         # MongoDB real-time statistics
# Essential for:# - Web application debugging
# - Database performance issues
# - Service connectivity problems

Container & Orchestration

# Docker and Kubernetes diagnosticsdocker stats                      # Container resource usage in real-time
docker logs -f container_name     # Follow container logs
kubectl get events --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp  # Kubernetes events timeline
kubectl top pods                  # Pod resource usage metrics
# These commands:# - Monitor container resource consumption
# - Debug application issues in containers
# - Track orchestration events

📜 Historical Analysis & Forensics

# Command history analysishistory | awk '{print $2}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10  # Most frequent commands
cat ~/.bash_history | grep -e "^ssh "  # Historical SSH connections
# File change tracking and timelinefind /etc -type f -name "*.conf" -exec stat -c %Y {} \; | sort -n | tail -1  # Last modified config
auditctl -w /etc/passwd -p wa -k passwd_changes  # Monitor passwd file for writes/accesses
# Critical for:# - Security incident investigation
# - User activity auditing
# - Change management verification

🚑 Emergency Recovery Commands

# Process and system debuggingstrace -p PID                      # Trace system calls of running process
ltrace -p PID                      # Trace library calls
gdb -p PID                         # Attach debugger to running process
systemctl --failed                 # List failed systemd services
journalctl -xb -p err..emerg      # Boot errors and critical messages
# File system recoveryfsck /dev/sda1                     # Filesystem check and repair
xfs_repair /dev/sdb1               # XFS filesystem repair
testdisk /dev/sdc                  # Partition table recovery
# Essential for:# - Application crash analysis# - Data recovery scenarios

🔐 Advanced Network Security

Network Security Deep Dive

# Port scanning and intrusion detectionnetstat -an | grep SYN_RECV | wc -l  # Count half-open connections (SYN flood)
ss -o state syn-recv -p             # SYN_RECV state connections with processes
# SSL/TLS security assessmentopenssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com  # TLS handshake debug
nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443 target.com  # Cipher suite enumeration
# Container network inspectionip netns list                       # List network namespaces
ip netns exec namespace_name netstat -tulpn  # Network stats within namespace
# Critical for:# - Network intrusion detection
# - SSL/TLS configuration validation
# - Container network troubleshooting

🛠️ Advanced Scripting Examples

System Health Monitor Script

#!/bin/bash# Comprehensive system health check
echo "=== $(date) System Health Check ==="echo "Uptime: $(uptime)"
echo "Load: $(cat /proc/loadavg)"
echo "Memory: $(free -h | grep Mem | awk '{print $3"/"$2}')"
echo "Disk: $(df -h / | awk 'NR==2 {print $4 " free"}')"
# Critical services checkservices=("sshd" "nginx" "mysql" "auditd")
for service in "${services[@]}"; do
    if systemctl is-active --quiet $service; then
        echo "✓ $service: ACTIVE"
    else
        echo "✗ $service: INACTIVE"
    fi
# Security checksecho "Failed login attempts: $(grep -c "Failed password" /var/log/auth.log 2>/dev/null || echo "N/A")"
echo "Current connections: $(ss -tun | wc -l)"
#!/bin/bash# Network security monitoring script
echo "=== Network Security Status ==="echo "Open listening ports:"
echo "Recent SSH failures:"grep "Failed password" /var/log/auth.log | tail -5
echo "Current iptables rules count:"iptables -L -n | wc -l
echo "Connection tracking:"conntrack -C 2>/dev/null || echo "conntrack not available"

💡 Interview Preparation Tips

Common Senior Linux Interview Questions:

  1. “How do you diagnose a server that’s running slow?”
  • Use the combination: top → pidstat → iostat → vmstat
  • Check: CPU steal time, I/O wait, memory pressure, swap activity
  1. “How do you investigate a security breach?”
  • Start with: lastb, ausearch, check auth.log, verify checksums
  • Use: rkhunter, chkrootkit, lsof -i, netstat -nap
  1. “How do you troubleshoot network connectivity issues?”
  • Follow the path: ping → mtr → ss → tcpdump
  • Check: DNS, routing, firewall rules, service status
  1. “How do you monitor file changes for compliance?”
  • Implement: auditd rules, find -mtime, file integrity monitoring
  • Use: AIDE, tripwire, or custom checksum scripts
  • The Linux Boot Process: BIOS → Bootloader → Kernel → Init → Runlevels
  • Process States: Running, Sleeping, Stopped, Zombie
  • Network Stack: Physical → Data Link → Network → Transport → Application
  • Memory Management: Physical vs Virtual memory, Swap, Page Cache
  • Filesystem Hierarchy: Standard directories and their purposes

📚 Additional Expert Commands

Systemd and Service Management

systemctl list-dependencies sshd    # Service dependenciessystemctl cat nginx                 # View service file content
systemctl status --failed           # All failed services
journalctl -u nginx --since "1 hour ago"  # Service logs for time period

Advanced Permissions and SELinux

getfacl /etc/shadow                 # File access control listssetfacl -m u:user:rw file           # Modify ACLs
ls -Z /var/www/html                 # SELinux contexts
chcon -t httpd_sys_content_t /path  # Change SELinux context
sestatus                            # SELinux statu
sysbench cpu run                    # CPU performance testfio --filename=/test.file --size=1GB --readwrite=randread  # Disk I/O test
iperf3 -c server_ip                 # Network bandwidth test
stress --cpu 4 --timeout 60s        # System stress test

Master these commands, understand the underlying concepts, and you’ll be prepared for any troubleshooting scenario or technical interview.

Remember: True expertise isn’t just about knowing commands — it’s about understanding system behaviour, recognizing patterns, and applying the right tool for each situation

items.title: Accessing and Monitoring Your Kubernetes Cluster: Exposing the Dashboard via Ingress with Minikube
items.author: Shahul H
items.content:
Monitoring Your Kubernetes Cluster: Exposing the Dashboard via Ingress with Minikube

In the previous article, we deployed MongoDB and Mongo Express on Minikube and learned how different Kubernetes components, such as Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, and Secrets, come together to build a working application.

Now that our cluster is running an application, wouldn’t it be nice to have a built-in way to what’s happening, monitor Pods, check logs, or restart Deployments visually?

That’s where the comes in, a web-based UI that lets you manage and observe your cluster right from your browser.

1. Introducing the Need for Ingress

When you first deploy an application in Kubernetes, the simplest way to make it accessible from your browser is by using a Service of type LoadBalancer or . This works great for quick tests, but it’s not ideal for production.

In a real-world setup, we want to expose our apps using a (like dashboard.com) and secure them with .

That’s exactly what does, it acts as a point into your cluster. Instead of opening multiple ports, Ingress routes traffic based on and to the correct internal services.

2. Understanding Ingress Conceptually

  • Ingress is not a Service, it’s a routing layer.
  • It requires an to function.
  • It evaluates rules and redirects requests to internal services.

An resource defines routing rules it says, “when a user visits dashboard.com, forward that request to the internal kubernetes-dashboard Service.”

However, an Ingress file alone doesn’t do anything. You also need an , which is a Pod running in your cluster that processes those rules and handles HTTP(S) traffic.

Ingress Setup in Cloud vs Bare Metal:

  • Leverages cloud provider’s load balancer (e.g., AWS, GCP) as the entry point.
  • Automatically integrates with Ingress controllers for easy setup.
  • Requires manual setup of a load balancer or proxy server as the entry point.
  • Options include software (e.g., HAProxy, Traefik) or hardware load balancers.

3. Configure Ingress in Minikube

There are different ways to configure that and to set it up, depending on which environment you are also which approach you choose. In our case we’re using Minikube to demonstrate on your local machine.

  1. Install Ingress Controller:
  • Use a third-party implementation (e.g., NGINX Ingress Controller).
minikube addons enable ingress

Verify the namespace:

In the Minikube cluster, kubernetes-dashboard already exists out of the box

kubectl get ns kubectl get all -n kubernetes-dashboard

2. Create an Ingress Resource:

  • Define rules for domain-based routing or path-based routing.

Let’s create a file: 

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1kind: Ingress  name: dashboard-ingress             # Name of the Ingress resource
  namespace: kubernetes-dashboard     # Where the Dashboard is installed
spec:    - host: dashboard.com             # Custom domain to access the dashboard        paths:
          - path: /                  # Route all paths under dashboard.com
            pathType: Prefix         # Prefix matching for the path              service:
                name: kubernetes-dashboard # Service name to route traffic to
                port:
                  number: 80           # Target port of the dashboard service

Apply the file: 

kubectl apply -f dashboard-ingress.yaml

3.

  • Map domain names (e.g., myapp.com) to the cluster’s entry point IP in the /etc/hosts file (for local testing) or via DNS.
Map the Domain (dashboard.com) to Minikube IP
1. kubectl get ingress -n kubernetes-dashboard [TO VERIFY THE IP]
2.Edit your /etc/hosts file to map dashboard.com to the Ingress IP:
Add this line at the bottom (get the IP from Step 1)
"192.168.64.5 dashboard.com"
Save and exit
5. Then access: http://dashboard.com

This way, your Kubernetes Dashboard remains internal (secured with ClusterIP), and only the Ingress exposes it through a friendly domain name.

4. Ingress Default Backend (Optional)

Every Ingress resource can optionally define a 

kubectl describe ingress dashboard-ingress -n kubernetes-dashboard

The acts as a “catch-all” for requests that don’t match any of your Ingress host or path rules.

This can be useful when you want to:

  • Return a for unknown domains or paths.
  • Redirect users to a or friendly fallback route.
  • Log or monitor invalid requests reaching your cluster.

In short, if the Ingress Controller receives a request for a hostname or path that isn’t defined, it forwards it to the default backend instead of dropping the request (like an Error 404).

By exposing the Kubernetes Dashboard through an Ingress, we learned not only how to manage our cluster visually but also how Ingress fits into the broader networking model of Kubernetes.

What’s the Difference Between a Service and an Ingress?

  • Exposes Pods internally within the cluster.
  • Service (NodePort / LoadBalancer): Exposes Pods externally by assigning a port or public IP.
  • Provides hostname and path-based routing on top of internal Services, allowing you to use custom domains and HTTPS.

What Is an Ingress Controller?

  • It’s a component that and actively watches for new or updated Ingress resources.
  • The controller and forwards external HTTP(S) traffic to the appropriate internal Services.
  • Example: The is commonly used in both Minikube and production environments.

Why Use a Default Backend?

  • It provides a for unknown requests instead of showing a generic “404 Not Found”.
  • You can serve a , , or for monitoring.
  • Especially useful in larger systems with many subdomains or complex routing rules.

items.title: Understanding Latency Differences Between AWS Storage Services — A Practical Guide
items.author: Harikrishnan M
items.content:
📖 Understanding Latency Differences Between AWS Storage Services — A Practical Guide 🚀

When architecting cloud solutions, one of the most overlooked but crucial factors is . In AWS, multiple storage services cater to different use cases — from low-latency block storage to highly scalable object storage.

But how do their compare? And more importantly — which one should you choose for your specific workload?

In this post, I’ll break down the real-world latency differences between popular AWS storage services like EBS, EFS, FSx, S3, and Instance Store — with practical use cases for each.

📊 AWS Storage Services — Latency Comparison Table

📌 Why Do These Latencies Differ?

Each AWS storage service is optimized for a specific access pattern and performance profile. Here’s a brief on why their latencies vary:

  • : Direct NVMe SSD attached locally to the instance — no network hop, hence ultra-low latency.
  • : Network-attached block storage — optimized for consistent sub-millisecond latency with io2 Block Express volumes.
  • : Managed file system over NFS protocol — involves network call and shared access overhead.
  • : HPC-grade file system designed for high concurrency and parallelism — low latency, optimized for large-scale data processing.
  • : Similar to EBS with SMB protocol overhead — slightly higher latency than block storage.
  • : Object storage accessed via HTTPS APIs — involves more networking layers and metadata lookup per object.

📌 Visual Latency Spectrum

A simple way to visualize this:

Instance Store < FSx for Lustre ≈ EBS io2 < FSx for Windows < EFS < S3

📌 Real-World Use Cases — When to Choose What

ScenarioRecommended StorageHigh-speed ephemeral storageUltra-low latency databasesShared POSIX-compliant file storageHPC, ML training, and Big DataWindows file shares and SMB workloadsFSx for Windows File ServerMassive object storage with scalability

📌 Pro Tip: Benchmark It Yourself 🚀

Want to measure real latencies in your AWS region? Use this quick setup:

  • Attach EBS volumes (gp3, io2)
  • Mount EFS and FSx file systems
  • Use fio tool for IOPS and latency testing
  • Use curl or AWS SDK for S3 GET/PUT latency measurements

If you’d like, I can share a Terraform + bash script combo for automated setup and benchmarking. Drop a comment if you want that! ✌️

Latency matters. A lot. Especially for workloads like databases, analytics, and high-frequency applications. Understanding the AWS storage service latency spectrum helps you make data-driven architecture decisions and optimize both performance and cost.

So next time you pick a storage backend in AWS, don’t just go by capacity or price — consider latency too.

📌 Follow for More AWS Deep-Dives ☁️🚀

If you found this post valuable, follow me here and on LinkedIn for more AWS, DevOps, and cloud infrastructure content.

Cheers!Harikrishnan M | Cloud DevOps Engineer

items.title: ipcofig in macOS, Linux | Get macOS IP address
items.author: Irteza Asad
items.content:
If you are a MacOS/Linux user who’s just switched from Windows and you absolutely hate the bloated output of ifconfig, this article is for you.

If you just want one command to get just your , this will do it for you. Copy it like this and execute it with multiple lines.

$ ifconfig | grep -E "([0-9]{1,3}\.){3}[0-9]{1,3}" \     | grep -v 127.0.0.1 | awk '{ print $2 }' | cut -f2 -d: | head -n1

You can get your address with thisAPI. Run the below command.

curl https://api.ipify.org\?format\=json

If you want the full ipconfig setup, keep reading.

For your very basic network information, you type ipconfig command on your Windows and you get something like this,

Very straightforward, readable information.

Now let’s run ifconfig on Mac,

And it goes on for about 100 more lines of information. No worries, the information you need is still in there……somewhere….

In this article, we’re going to get the IP address on Mac/Linux with ipconfig. Let’s get started.

In it’s in the root(~) directory. Paste this block of code at the bottom of the file and save the file.

network_info() {  local interface="$1"
  # Check if the interface name is provided
  if [ -z "$interface" ]; then
    echo "Usage: parse_network_info <INTERFACE_NAME>"
    return 1
  # Use ifconfig to retrieve network information
  local ifconfig_output
  ifconfig_output=$(ifconfig "$interface" 2>/dev/null)
  # Check if the interface exists and is active
  if [ -z "$ifconfig_output" ]; then
    echo "Interface '$interface' not found or not active."
    return 1
  # Parse and display IPv4 address, netmask, and default gateway
  local ipv4_address
  ipv4_address=$(echo "$ifconfig_output" | grep -oE 'inet (addr:)?[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+' | awk '{print $2}')
  netmask=$(echo "$ifconfig_output" | grep -o 'netmask 0x[0-9a-f]*' | awk '{print $2}' | cut -c3- | awk '{gsub(/^0[xX]/,""); print}')  default_gateway=$(route -n get default | grep 'gateway:' | awk '{print $2}')
  # Display the parsed network information
  echo "Interface...............: $interface"
  echo "IPv4 Address............: $ipv4_address"
  echo "Subnet Mask.............: $netmask"
  echo "Default Gateway.........: $default_gateway"
}

In this simple script, we’re just parsing the ifconfig information and presenting it in a similar manner as ipconfig from Windows.

Let’s quit and reopen (a restart is needed to apply the changes) the bash and run this command, network_info eth0

$ network_info eth0Interface...............: eth0
IPv4 Address............: 192.168.0.102
Subnet Mask.............: 255.255.255.0
Default Gateway.........: 192.168.0.1

If you don’t have a way to restart bash, just run source ~/.bashrc before you run the network_info eth0 command.

For , we’re going to follow the same procedure. We will open the  file located in the root(~) directory. But As ifconfig in MacOS gives us the in formal we need to convert it to first. Our script for MacOS would be,

hex_to_decimal_ip() {  local hex_ip="$1"
  # Check if a hexadecimal IP is provided
  if [ -z "$hex_ip" ]; then
    echo "Usage: hex_to_decimal_ip <HEX_IP>"
    return 1
  # Remove '0x' prefix if present
  hex_ip="${hex_ip#0x}"
  # Split the hexadecimal IP into four 2-character segments
  local seg1="${hex_ip:0:2}"
  local seg2="${hex_ip:2:2}"
  local seg3="${hex_ip:4:2}"
  local seg4="${hex_ip:6:2}"
  # Convert each segment from hexadecimal to decimal
  local decimal_seg1=$((16#$seg1))
  local decimal_seg2=$((16#$seg2))
  local decimal_seg3=$((16#$seg3))
  local decimal_seg4=$((16#$seg4))
  # Create the decimal IP address by joining the segments with dots
  local decimal_ip="$decimal_seg1.$decimal_seg2.$decimal_seg3.$decimal_seg4"}  local interface="$1"
  # Check if the interface name is provided
  if [ -z "$interface" ]; then
    echo "Usage: parse_network_info <INTERFACE_NAME>"
    return 1
  # Use ifconfig to retrieve network information
  local ifconfig_output
  ifconfig_output=$(ifconfig "$interface" 2>/dev/null)
  # Check if the interface exists and is active
  if [ -z "$ifconfig_output" ]; then
    echo "Interface '$interface' not found or not active."
    return 1
  # Parse and display IPv4 address, netmask, and default gateway
  local ipv4_address
  ipv4_address=$(echo "$ifconfig_output" | grep -oE 'inet (addr:)?[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+' | awk '{print $2}')
  netmask=$(echo "$ifconfig_output" | grep -o 'netmask 0x[0-9a-f]*' | awk '{print $2}' | cut -c3- | awk '{gsub(/^0[xX]/,""); print}')  default_gateway=$(route -n get default | grep 'gateway:' | awk '{print $2}')
  # Display the parsed network information
  echo "Interface...............: $interface"
  echo "IPv4 Address............: $ipv4_address"
  echo "Subnet Mask.............: $(hex_to_decimal_ip $netmask)"
  echo "Default Gateway.........: $default_gateway"
}

Let’s run network_info en0,

$ network_info en0Interface...............: en0
IPv4 Address............: 192.168.0.102
Subnet Mask.............: 255.255.255.0
Default Gateway.........: 192.168.0.1

Ok, we’ve done the hard part, now we will simply create to run this when we type in ipconfig.

Open up your .bashrc again and paste this line at the very bottom, after the network_info function,

alias ipconfig='echo "$(network_info en0)"'

Save the file. This is the same for both MacOS and Linux.

$ ipconfigInterface...............: en0
IPv4 Address............: 192.168.0.102
Subnet Mask.............: 255.255.255.0
Default Gateway.........: 192.168.0.1

Hope you enjoyed this small tutorial. See you on the next one ❤️

items.title: Setting Up a Secure TURN Server (Coturn) for WebRTC — Like the Ones Behind WhatsApp Calls
items.author: Muhammad Abdullah
items.content:
Setting Up a Secure TURN Server (Coturn) for WebRTC — Like the Ones Behind WhatsApp Calls

If you’ve ever built a voice or video calling feature using WebRTC, you’ve probably heard of . They’re the unsung heroes that make your calls connect — even when users are behind strict firewalls, mobile networks, or corporate NATs.

In fact, this same technology is what powers WhatsApp Business calling, , , and many other real-time communication systems. Let’s see how to set it up yourself — securely, privately, and production-ready.

What is a TURN Server and Why You Need It

TURN (Traversal Using Relays around NAT) is part of the . When two users try to connect, the browser first attempts a direct peer-to-peer link via a STUN server. But when both peers are behind restrictive NATs, this fails — that’s where TURN comes in.

The TURN server relays audio/video traffic between both users, ensuring the call still goes through.

  • Calls may fail for users on 4G/5G or enterprise Wi-Fi.
  • File transfer and data channels may hang or never start.

That’s why apps like , , and rely on TURN in the background — it’s the bridge that makes real-time communication reliable.

Step 1: Prepare Your Environment

  • A Kubernetes cluster (EKS, GKE, or K3s)
  • A DNS record like turn.example.com pointing to your node or load balancer
  • A Kubernetes secret for credentials

Create your secret (this keeps sensitive data out of YAML files):

kubectl create secret generic turn-secret \  --from-literal=TURN_USERNAME=myturnuser \
  --from-literal=TURN_PASSWORD=supersecurepassword \
  -n namespace

Step 2: Coturn Pod Configuration

Below is a clean, production-ready manifest stripped of all runtime metadata:

apiVersion: v1kind: Pod  name: coturn  labels:spec:  dnsPolicy: ClusterFirstWithHostNet    - name: coturn
      image: coturn/coturn:4.7.0
      imagePullPolicy: Always        - -n        - --fingerprint        - --realm=your-domain.com
        - --user=$(TURN_USERNAME):$(TURN_PASSWORD)
        - --listening-port=3478        - --max-port=65535
        - --external-ip=YOUR_PUBLIC_IP/YOUR_INTERNAL_IP
      ports:          containerPort: 3478          protocol: UDP        - name: TURN_USERNAME            secretKeyRef:              key: TURN_USERNAME          valueFrom:              name: turn-secret  restartPolicy: Always
kubectl apply -f coturn-pod.yaml

Once it’s running, your TURN server is live and ready.

Step 3: Hiding Sensitive Details

You don’t want to expose your domain name, node IP, or credentials publicly — here’s how to keep them private:

  1. Use a Load Balancer or CloudFront proxy to mask your node’s real IP.
  2. , such as relay.example.net, instead of your app domain.
  3. to prevent credentials from showing up in stdout.
  4. Always use Kubernetes Secrets to inject environment variables — never hardcode credentials.

If you’re deploying on AWS, put the Coturn node behind a Network Load Balancer (NLB) or assign an to keep the node private but reachable.

Step 4: Configure Your App Environment

Now set your TURN configuration in your application or .env file:

TURN_URL=turn:relay.example.net:3478TURN_USERNAME=myturnuser
TURN_PASSWORD=supersecurepassword

Then add it to your WebRTC configuration:

const pc = new RTCPeerConnection({  iceServers: [      urls: 'turn:relay.example.net:3478',      credential: TURN_PASSWORD  ]

Your users can now connect through your TURN relay — even behind NATs or corporate firewalls.

Step 5: Optional Hardening and Scaling

  • Convert this Pod into a for automatic recovery and scaling.
  • Expose via a (NodePort or LoadBalancer).
  • Use for encrypted TURN traffic.
  • Restrict allowed ports in your firewall to only what Coturn needs (3478, 49152–65535).
  • Add monitoring (Prometheus or Grafana) to track connections and bandwidth.

How This Compares to WhatsApp Business Calling

When you make a WhatsApp Business voice or video call, it uses the same underlying WebRTC structure — . TURN ensures calls can traverse complex networks and always find a working route between users.

The main difference? Large-scale systems like WhatsApp use distributed TURN servers across regions and load-balance based on latency.

But the concept — and even much of the configuration — remains the same as the one you just deployed.

Setting up a TURN server like Coturn isn’t complicated, but it’s critical. It’s the invisible infrastructure that guarantees connectivity across every kind of network.

By running it on Kubernetes, hiding sensitive data, and securing your deployment, you’re following the same engineering practices that power real-time apps like WhatsApp, Google Meet, and Zoom.

Your users will never know it’s there — but their calls will always connect.

items.title: How I Designed a Multi-Cloud Kubernetes Disaster Recovery Setup After the AWS Outage
items.author: Athar
items.content:
A practical guide to building a cost-efficient multi-cloud DR setup using AWS, GCP, Argo CD, and smart database syncing.

Learn how I (Athar, a DevOps engineer) built a multi-cloud Kubernetes disaster recovery setup after the recent AWS outage — using AWS + GCP, Argo CD, DNS failover, and cost-optimization tricks that keep standby infrastructure cheap but ready.

When AWS went down recently, I saw how dependent we all still are on a single cloud provider. I’m , a DevOps engineer with about four years of experience, and that outage was my cue to design a proper multi-cloud Kubernetes disaster recovery setup — one that doesn’t drain money while sitting idle.

This post walks through exactly how I built it: from Kubernetes clusters and database syncing to DNS failover and cost optimization. Simple architecture, no over-engineering — just practical design that actually works.

Why Multi-Cloud DR Became Non-Optional

AWS is stable, but no platform is perfect. When services like S3 or EC2 in one region fail, it’s not just downtime — it’s panic for the whole pipeline.

I wanted a setup that could:

  • Keep a in another cloud (GCP).
  • Sync data, container images, and configs daily.
  • Recover full functionality fast.
  • Avoid paying for idle compute and databases.

The idea was simple: resilience without constant spend.

ComponentPrimary (AWS)Secondary (GCP)ComputeEKS (active)GKE (standby)StorageS3 bucketGCS bucket (daily sync)DatabaseRDSCloud SQL (cold standby)CD ToolArgo CDArgo CD (synced setup)DNSRoute 53Cloud DNS

AWS runs production. GCP stays on standby — synced but powered down.

  • using rclone or native transfer jobs.
  • Transactional database sync keeps Cloud SQL close to real-time.

This ensures GCP is ready to pick up whenever AWS stumbles.

Kubernetes Sync with Argo CD

Argo CD manages both clusters from the same Git repo. Only the AWS cluster stays active; the GCP cluster is scaled to zero.

When failover happens, Argo CD in GCP detects it (through health checks or manual trigger) and spins up nodes automatically. All deployments, secrets, and configs stay identical because Git is the single source of truth.

That means no drift, no surprises, and no panic.

Database Strategy (and Cost Control)

Running two databases 24/7? Too expensive. Here’s the workaround: GCP’s Cloud SQL instance stays stopped most of the time.

A handles the rest:

  1. Restores the latest RDS snapshot from S3.
  2. Applies transactional deltas since the last backup.
  3. Keeps a fresh copy ready for activation.

So when AWS is down, you start Cloud SQL, run the update, and it’s ready — without paying all month for idle compute.

Storage stays cheap. Data stays fresh. Compute stays off.

For S3 and GCS, daily sync jobs keep the data mirrored. This ensures that static files, logs, and backups remain accessible on both sides.

If you need tighter RTOs, make it hourly — but for most setups, daily syncs strike the right balance between cost and readiness.

DNS Failover with Short TTLs

DNS TTLs are set to in Route 53. If AWS fails, a DNS update flips traffic to GCP’s ingress IPs.

Users reconnect in minutes. For even smoother failovers, tools like can sync records between Route 53 and Cloud DNS automatically.

Cost Optimization — The Main Win

A true DR setup shouldn’t double your bill. Here’s how this one stays lean:

  • GCP cluster nodes are when idle.
  • handle non-critical jobs.
  • Cloud SQL remains stopped until failover.
  • handle syncs and automation.

This cuts costs down to roughly 20% of an active-active setup, without losing reliability.

Lessons from the AWS Outage

The AWS outage proved that “single cloud” equals “single point of failure.” A good DR plan isn’t about fancy tools — it’s about having and .

With Argo CD managing syncs, serverless tools handling data updates, and standby resources ready to wake up, my setup can rebuild everything in .

No wasted compute. No expensive idle infrastructure. Just smart, fast recovery.

If you’re running production on a single cloud, start small but start now.Disaster recovery doesn’t need to be expensive — it just needs to be designed right.

  • GitOps-based Kubernetes syncs
  • Transactional database updates

That’s all it takes to make your system truly resilient — and save money while doing it.

DevOps Engineer | Cloud + Automation Enthusiast

At the dawn of the first ever FluxCon, Andy will share his perspective on Flux’s strong community, broad adoption, both expert-led and “DIY,” and a clear intent to make GitOps the foundation for secure, compliant, and scalable infrastructure and software delivery. In the opening of the keynote, he’ll deliver key project updates, highlighting recent achievements and offering a glimpse of what’s on the roadmap.

Flux’s adoption is evolving from a deployment engine into a governance control tower for software delivery, ensuring compliance, security, and trust across environments. Andy will also introduce the vision behind Flux’s MCP, laying the foundation for AI-assisted GitOps. This will bring even greater clarity and control to complex systems and configurations. As part of this, Andy will reference the AI reference architecture and plans to evolve Flux MCP.

items.title: Building a Unified SAST + DAST Security Pipeline (Our Next Step in DevSecOps)
items.author: Rajesh Thakur
items.content:
DevOps Engineer @

But code is only half the story.

Applications behave differently once deployed. Redirects change, cookies behave differently, headers appear or disappear, and hidden endpoints surface. In other words:

✔ The real security story begins when the app is running.

That’s why this week, we took the next major step in our DevSecOps journey by integrating Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST) using in our staging environment — completing the SAST + DAST pipeline.

🔍 Why We Needed DAST (Even After SAST Was Done)

SAST is great at catching issues in code:

    • Missing or weak security headers
    • Runtime misconfigurations
    • Issues caused by load balancers or reverse proxies

    These appear only when the application is running.

    DAST is the runtime lens SAST cannot provide.

    🛠 What We Implemented: DAST Using OWASP ZAP

    We integrated a that automatically runs whenever a build is deployed to staging:

    • Safe for staging environments
    • Detects real-world weaknesses without risk

    This ensures we catch runtime weaknesses early — before they ever reach production.

    🔄 The Combined SAST + DAST Pipeline (Architecture Diagram)

    Here’s a clean, Medium-friendly architecture diagram showing how everything now fits together:

    ┌────────────────────────┐                   │        Developers       │
                       └─────────────┬──────────┘
                                     │  Push Code              ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
                  │           CI/CD Pipeline             │
                  └───────┬────────────┬────────────────┘                      │             │
            ┌─────────────▼──┐     ┌───▼─────────────────┐
            │      SAST       │     │        SCA           │
            │ (Semgrep,       │     │ (Dependencies,       │
            │  Gitleaks)      │     │  Vulnerable libs)    │
            └─────────────┬───┘     └──────────┬──────────┘
                          │                    │                                 ▼                        │ RAW FINDINGS   │                        └───────┬────────┘                                ▼                     │ Normalization Layer │                     │  Deduplicate + Map) │                             │
                   ┌─────────────▼──────────────┐
                   │  Automated Ticketing        │
                   │ (Create only real issues)   │
                   └─────────────┬──────────────┘                             ▼                    │   Dashboards      │                    └───────────────────┘
                    ┌──────────────────────────┐                │      STAGING ENV         │
                    └─────────────┬────────────┘
                                  │                    ┌───────────────────┐                    │ (Baseline Scan)   │                             │ Findings                        (Feeds into

    This diagram shows clearly:

    • SAST → SCA → Unified Findings → Normalization
    • DAST → Normalization → Ticketing → Dashboards
    • All components feeding into ONE security intelligence pipeline

    ⚠️ What DAST Immediately Uncovered

    Within minutes of enabling ZAP, we found issues SAST never flagged:

    • Cookies missing HttpOnly and Secure
    • Redirect chains leaking path info
    • A couple of unintended exposed endpoints
    • Header mismatches between staging and production

    This validated exactly why DAST is necessary.

    Now our DevSecOps pipeline has:

    ✔ Code analysis (SAST) ✔ Dependency analysis (SCA)

    ✔ Runtime analysis (DAST)

    ✔ A unified normalization engine ✔ Continuous monitoring

    ✔ Developer-friendly visibility

    This is no longer “just scanning.” This is a Security Intelligence Platform.

    🧩 GitHub Actions YAML Example for OWASP ZAP DAST

    Here is a clean, ready-to-use YAML workflow for running ZAP Baseline Scan in GitHub Actions:

    on:  workflow_dispatch:    branches:  pull_request:
    jobs:  zap_scan:
        steps:    - name: Checkout repo
          uses: actions/checkout@v3
        - name: Run OWASP ZAP Baseline Scan      uses: zaproxy/action-baseline@v0.7.0        target: "https://your-staging-url.com"
            rules_file_name: ".zap/rules.tsv"
            cmd_options: "-a"
        - name: Upload ZAP Report      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3        name: zap-report

    Modify target: to match your staging URL.

    ✔ create the .zap/rules.tsv file✔ integrate authenticated scanning

    ✔ push findings directly to Jira or GitHub issues✔ merge SAST + DAST results into one JSON output

    In the next phase, I’ll be:

    • Building authenticated DAST flows
    • Adding automated severity-based blocking
    • Visualizing DAST + SAST + SCA in a single dashboard
    • Scoring services based on risk level

    More diagrams and updates coming soon.

    DevOps Engineer @ 📞 +91 7018188377

    #DevOps #AWS #CloudComputing #DevSecOps #Security #Automation #SAST #DAST #Deploycrafts www.deploycrafts.com
